description: On the other side of the body, 'kuri' identifies the right. Without a possessor, the word normally takes the speaker's body as its anchor; 'thia kuri' means your right. The word remains spatial; correctness, moral justification, and entitlement use other Phi vocabulary.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into stressed high-back rounded 'u'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip at the alveolar ridge for 'r', then finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
usage_notes: 'mua kuri' locates something on the speaker's right unless a possessor reanchors the scene; 'kuri ruela' is the right path. Its sound deliberately stays far from 'lawe' (left).

examples.translation: The house is on my right.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Keeping direction separate from correctness prevents a spatial term from lending moral weight to one side.

semantic_domains.physical: the right side of an oriented body
semantic_domains.spatial: a body-relative side extended to an oriented scene
